# Excalidraw Data

## Text Elements
The Quantum Circuit Model ^Xp5YJce9

logical bits and quantum gates ^PrzgTwWl

n qubits carried by n wires ^4ZBiNm9P

n other wires carry the output qubits ^AM0ia0Yf

we will restrict attention to unitary quantum gates ^sTBMr96L

non-unitary quantum operations can be simulated
by unitary quantum gates if we allow the
possibility of adding an ancilla and of discarding
some output bits ^wrlI2SrX

quantum wire carrying the quantum state resulting
from the measurement ^U8wwLQnv

often the quantum outcome
is discarded or ignored ^tdkucx82

omit the wire from the drawing ^borYdy8w

Quantum Gates ^PK1jwLZJ

any unitary operator acting on a 2-dimensional
quantum system (a qubit) is called a '1-qubit
quantum gate' ^WVSKeYgZ

every 1-qubit pure state is represented as a point
on the surface of the Bloch sphere, or equivalently
as a a unit vector whose origin is fixed at the
centre of the Bloch sphere ^0HeAUlkg

the action of U on      can be thought of as a
rotation of the Bloch vector for     to the Bloch
vector for ^bfqpOHq4

if we exponentiate the Pauli gates we gate unitary
operators corresponding to very important classes
of 1-qubit gates ^2N789wvB

rotation gates ^EMyOdhxB

rotation about the x-, y-, and z- axes of the
Bloch sphere ^vl6wlrlQ

the rotation gates are defined as follows ^wkICTFRm

Theorem ^h5OyVB4L

Suppose U is a 1-qubit unitary gate. Then there
exist real numbers             and    such that ^NEOtsW8Y

we can decompose any 1-qubit gate into a sequence
of two rotations about the z-axis and one
rotation about the y-axis, along with a suitable
phase factor ^Uh1GbC5y

we can also give decompositions of 1-qubit gates in
terms of rotations about any other two
non-parallel axes of the Bloch sphere ^DebvPyhH

Theorem ^91fbIXsY

Suppose U is a 1-qubit unitary gate. Let   and   be
any two non-parallel axes of the Bloch sphere.
Then there exist real numbers          and   such
that ^g4oGbY1K

Corollary ^CqRCGgc0

any 1-qubit gate U can be written in the form ^JhPXuLjd

where A, B, C are unitary operators satisfying ^RpVDURMw

given any 1-qubit gate U, we can similarly define a
controlled-U gate, denoted c-U, which will be a 
2-qubit gate corresponding to ^QO0XTuIh

given a circuit    implementing a unitary U, the
basis technique to implement a circuit for the
controlled-U operation is to replace every gate G
in    by a controlled gate c-G ^waS1YxoS
